coredata.dc:title: Dispersion of optical and structural properties in gel column separated carbon nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Kokorina, Alina A.
Prikhozhdenko, Ekaterina S.
Tarakina, Nadezda V.
Sapelkin, Andrei V.
Sukhorukov, Gleb B.
Goryacheva, Irina Y.
coredata.dc:description: We report an effective and simple method for separation of carbon nanodots (CNDs) by size-exclusion chromatography that yields CNDs fractions with different sizes and luminescent properties. As a result, three major types of CNDs with spectrally distinct luminescent centers were identified. The first type of luminescent particles has emission maxima at about 400–440 nm, the highest size and luminescence intensity. CNDs of the second type have a middle size and the lowest luminescence intensity with emission maxima in the range 450–470 nm. The distinctive feature of these two fractions is the excitation-dependent shift of the emission maxima to longer wavelengths. The third type of CNDs has luminescence around 520 nm with no emission shift upon the increase of excitation wavelength and the smallest size. The observed spectral variations in different fractions correlate with a change in average particle size as seen in TEM images. Size fractionation of CNDs mixture is very important to many fields, mostly for bioimaging and medicine applications because it leads to CNDs' unified distribution and behavior. Dilution of CNDs in water suspension leads to the increase of emission intensity and shifts the PL spectra to shorter wavelengths providing evidence for concentration quenching effect in CNDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Gel column chromatography separation of CNDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Russian Science foundation (project 16-13-10195 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of hydrothermal synthesis of luminescent CNDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
a) PL spectra of obtained CNDs solution; b) PL spectra of 8-fold dilution CNDs solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
The dependence of intensity (a) and emission maxima position (b) on the excitation wavelength for different CNDs water solution dilutions.

PL spectra of fractions with different retention volume: a – 7 ml (fraction10); b - 10.5 ml (fraction15); c- 16.1 ml (fraction 23); d- 25.9 ml (fraction 37); e− 27.3 ml (fraction 39). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
Raman spectra of the fractions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
The dependence of intensity (left) and emission maxima position (right) from excitation wavelength for fractions 10 (a), 23 (b), 39 (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

High-resolution TEM images of carbon dotes from fractions 5 (a), 15 (c), 23 (e) and 38 (g). Particle size distribution for the fractions 5 (b), 15 (d) and 23 (f). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

head.ce:title: Dispersion of optical and structural properties in gel column separated carbon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Saratov State University, 83 Astrakhanskaya Street, Saratov, 410012, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Queen Mary University of London, Mile End Road, E1 4NS, UK

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Max Planck Institute of Colloids and Interfaces, Am Mühlenberg 1, Potsdam, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Max Planck Institute of Colloids and Interfaces

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report an effective and simple method for separation of carbon nanodots (CNDs) by size-exclusion chromatography that yields CNDs fractions with different sizes and luminescent properties. As a result, three major types of CNDs with spectrally distinct luminescent centers were identified. The first type of luminescent particles has emission maxima at about 400–440 nm, the highest size and luminescence intensity. CNDs of the second type have a middle size and the lowest luminescence intensity with emission maxima in the range 450–470 nm. The distinctive feature of these two fractions is the excitation-dependent shift of the emission maxima to longer wavelengths. The third type of CNDs has luminescence around 520 nm with no emission shift upon the increase of excitation wavelength and the smallest size. The observed spectral variations in different fractions correlate with a change in average particle size as seen in TEM images. Size fractionation of CNDs mixture is very important to many fields, mostly for bioimaging and medicine applications because it leads to CNDs' unified distribution and behavior. Dilution of CNDs in water suspension leads to the increase of emission intensity and shifts the PL spectra to shorter wavelengths providing evidence for concentration quenching effect in CNDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a new and unique class of luminescent nanoparticles. CNDs have many advantages such as their small size around units of nanometers [1–3], high surface area, special morphology, unique electronic, optical characteristics [4], biocompatibility and chemical inertness; they mainly consist of non-toxic C, O and N atoms. These important characteristics are potentially promising in biological labeling [5], biosensing [6,7], photocatalysis [8,9].

Another advantage of CNDs is a large variety of carbon sources to produce the desired product such as carbon materials with different structure (graphene, graphite rod etc.) [10,11], carbohydrates (dextran, glucose, sucrose, starch, citric acid) [12,13], natural products like coffee [14], banana juice [15], orange waste peels [16], and many other.

In addition, there is a possibility of synthesis with a range of chemical and physical methods such as hydrothermal synthesis [13], microwave synthesis [17], electrochemical synthesis [10,11], laser ablation, metal-graphite intercalation [18], pyrolysis in concentrated acid and carbonization in a microreactors [19] 9 etc. Many of these ways of CNDs synthesis allow creating photoluminescent (PL) nanoparticles with different structure, they are fast, cheap and usually without using strong reagents such as concentrated acids, organic solvents and so on.

Today the physical origin of PL and morphology of carbon nanoscale materials is still unclear, with numerous fundamental questions yet unresolved [20]. It is believed, that presence of surface defects in carbon systems is one of the main cause of CNDs PL properties [5,20–22]. Unlike luminescent semiconductor quantum dots (such as CdSe) where PL is largely determined by carrier (electron and hole) confinement, carbon nanoparticles have a different luminescence mechanism with functional groups on CNDs surface playing the key role. The CNDs surface does not consist of isolated chemical groups but rather the hybridization of the carbon backbone and connected chemical groups [23]. Hybridized carbons (sp2 and sp3) and other functionalized surface defects, such as carbonyl-related localized electronic states are present in CNDs and contribute to the light emission. Thus, the bright surface defect-derived PL of CNDs is due to the recombination of electron–hole pairs in π and π* electronic levels of the sp2 sites. These sites lie between the bandgap of the σ and σ * states of the sp3 matrix, leading to strong visible emissions [24]. Furthermore, a higher degree of surface oxidation can result in more surface defects, resulting in the red-shifted emission [25]. Thus, the comprehensive and effective theoretical description developed for conventional quantum dots is not directly applicable to CNDs. Moreover, their PL response is not collective and represents a composition of individual emitters [20].

One of common features of CNDs is the dependence of the emission wavelength on the excitation wavelength. The main reason for that is the simultaneous presence luminescent centers with the different size and morphology [23,26,27]. The use of various synthetic methods allows obtaining the mixture of CNDs with different size and structure. There are various approaches to the separation and purification of CNDs such as centrifugation (after removal of soot) [28,29] dialysis [10,30], electrophoresis [1], chromatographic methods [6,14,31] including gel column chromatography [32]. Gel column chromatography allows purification of carbon nanoparticles from substrate soot, extraction of CNDs fractions of maximum quantum yield [33] and separation of CNDs fractions by size [32]. However, approaches described in literature so far do not yield CNDs separation that results in distinct PL spectral characteristic.

In this work, we report an effective size separation method to separate polydisperse CNDs solution into fractions by gel column chromatography for further detailed research. This method allows to quickly and easily isolate CNDs fractions with different size, morphology and to determine the effect of CNDs size on the position of maxima and luminescence intensity. The resulted structures divided on fractions are analyzed by luminescence, Raman spectroscopy and TEM. The obtainment of more size-homogenous fractions allows easy control of CNDs properties in many areas, including application in vivo.

Dextran sulfate sodium salt (DSS, Mw∼ 70 kDa) was purchased from BioChemika. CNDs were synthesized directly from DSS using hydrothermal synthesis in bidistilled water. The typical procedure includes preparation of DSS water solution (6 ml) 0.042 g (0.6 pmol). The solution was then transferred into a glass cup, placed into the teflon cup with tight-fitting cover, put into stainless steel autoclave and heated at 220 °C for 3 h. The resulting solution was cooled at room temperature and the uplayer solution containing product was centrifuged (20 min, 7500 g).

Desalting column PD-10 with Sephadex G-25 medium (GE Healthcare, UK) was used. First, equilibration buffer was removed and 25 ml of bidistillate flew through the column. In the next step we add 2.5 ml of CNDs water solution into the column and remove the first 2.5 ml of solution that drop from the column. The third step is adding 35 ml of bidistallate water in 5 ml portions. We have collected 48 CNDs fractions by 700 μl.

Luminescent spectra were measured with a spectrofluorimeter Cary Eclipse (Agilent Technologies, Australia). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL 2010 transmission electron microscope (Jeol, Japan) operated at 200 kV at the Nanovision center at QMUL. For TEM measurements each studied fraction of CNDs was kept in an ultrasonic bath for 3 min and then dropcasted to an ultrathin carbon film supported on a Cu grid and left to dry. Raman spectra of CDs after autoclaving were acquired with a Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer (Renishaw UK) under 532 nm irradiation (200 μW). The integration time was 30 s per single spectrum.

We determined that dilution of CNDs water solutions leads to change of emission intensity (Fig. 3 a) and shifts the PL spectra to shorter wavelengths (Fig. 3b) CNDs water solution before dilution has emission maximum around 510 nm, after double dilution with water the maximum shifts to 490 nm with low intensity increase. Further dilutions result in reduction of emission wavelengths by 60 nm, and excitation wavelength for these solutions remains constant (340 nm) up to 128-fold dilution. Shift of emission with dilution to a shorter wavelength area could be caused by a decrease in the photon reabsorption probability with a decreasing of CNDs concentration [34]. We found that CNDs solution has maximum PL intensity after being diluted 8-fold with water (see Fig. 3a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gel column chromatography separation of CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the synthesized CNDs sample was fractionated on a Sephadex G-25 gel column aiming to obtain fractions with different properties. As a result of CNDs solution separation, we received 48 CNDs fractions (each of 0.7 ml) with varied luminescence properties. Fraction number 1 passes the column first following higher number of fraction with number 48 being last one.

PL study of each fraction was carried out using different excitation wavelengths for detailed exploration of optical properties of obtained CNDs fractions. The results of excitation-dependent PL measurements for a number of selected fractions are shown in Fig. 4. 3D PL spectra are presented in Supporting materials (Fig. S2).

We can broadly identify three PL bands associated with the synthesized CNDs that can be observed in different fractions. The first type (type 1) of emitters has emission maxima about 400–440 nm and appears in fractions 8–15 (Fig. 4 a, Fig S2a). The maximum of luminescence intensity is reached by 340 nm excitation wavelength, further increase of excitation wavelength leads to reduction of luminescence intensity. PL bands of emitters of type 1 have the shift of emission maxima to longer wavelengths as a distinctive feature of CNDs. There is a formation of the second prominent emission maxima above 500 nm as excitation wavelength is increased above 330–340 nm. This fact may indicate the presence of several CNDs with different molecular structure (Fig. 4a, Fig. S2a).

The second type of emitters (type 2) starts to appear in fractions 10–19. PL spectra of fraction 15 are presented on Fig. 4b, Fig S2b. It is interesting to mention, the PL of type I is dominant at excitation up to 380 nm (maximum at 360 nm). Emission above 400 nm results in the PL of type 2. On the base of the appearance of two PL maxima, we speculate that fraction 15 includes two CNDs types, most likely with different surface properties, size and/or morphology. We also noticed that in these fractions, both for type 1 and type 2, there is almost no shift of the emission wavelength upon the increase of excitation wavelengths what probably indicates of CNDs of the same sort.

Upon the increase of fraction number (21–33) the contribution of the particles responsible for the short-wave emission maximum (type 1) reduces, and by fraction 23 it completely disappears. We assume this indicates presence of only the type 2 of emitters in these fractions. The fractions 21–33 show the shift of the emission wavelengths upon the increase of excitation wavelengths that is typical for carbon nanoparticles (Fig. 4c, Fig. S2c).

The third type of emitters (type 3) is characterized by long-wavelength emission maximum at 530 nm and it appears by fraction 35. The maximum luminescence intensity (excitation wavelength 500 nm) of these emission maxima is reached by fraction 39 and then these maxima gradually reduced in intensity while proceeding to fraction 48. The distinctive feature of PL spectrum for third type of molecular structures is no shift of emission wavelength upon the increase of excitation wavelength. This is probably due to the presence of luminescent centers of a similar type (Fig. 4d, Fig S2d).

In order to correlate PL data with the underlying structure and morphology of CNDs we carried out Raman measurements of each fraction since in low dimensional carbon systems, Raman spectra can provide wealth of information on the level of structural ordering [35,36]. Results of Raman measurements are shown in Fig. 5 . In all cases the signals contain peaks that can be assigned to D (around 1330 cm−1) and G (around 1590 cm−1) bands characteristic of carbon materials. In addition, one can see evolution of the Raman signal between fractions with two clear trends: (i) increase in the ratio of intensity between D and G bands (I(D)/I(G)) and (ii) emergence of a side peak at around 1475 cm−1. Widths of D and G bands suggest high level of disorder and/or clustering in CNDs. Relatively high position (1590 cm−1) of the G band compared to that in graphite (1580 cm−1) is an indication of small particle size and is consistent with the TEM data. At the same time, peak fitting analysis (performed using Lorentzian functions) does not reveal regular pattern in the evolution of the G band position in 10–39 fraction sequence. D band originates from aromatic rings [34] and its width defined by the ring ordering. Again, we observe no regular pattern in variation of D band width between the fractions.

On the other hand, increase in the intensity ratio of D and G peaks (I(D)/I(G)) suggests reduction of CND particle size and an increase in proportion of sp
3 sites. Finally, the band around 1475 cm−1 is most likely associated [37] with a variety of hydrocarbons (CH3, CH2) present in the system. The latter is most prominent in the higher fractions (see Fig. 5), which is consistent with the smaller cluster size (also confirmed by TEM measurements described below) and hence an increase of the contribution of surface groups to the Raman signal. Variations of surface groups across various fractions will certainly affect efficiency of available PL emission channels resulting in complex PL picture we observe. In particular, two of the emission channels can be attributed to the structures consisting of disordered aromatic carbon clusters and the hydrocarbon surface groups.

Fig. 6 shows the summarized information on the dependence of intensity and emission maxima position from excitation wavelength for three CNDs types. CNDs of the first type (Fig. 6a) have the highest PL intensity. The increase of excitation wavelengths for this CNDs type leads to bathochromic shift of the emission maxima. CNDs of the second type (Fig. 6b) have the lowest luminescence intensity; there is also a shift of the emission band in relation to the excitation band. The third type of CNDs (Fig. 6c) has intensive luminescent bands in the area of 520 nm, and there is no shift of the emission maxima. We assume that particles of the third type have similar morphology.

TEM images of CNDs from fractions 5, 15, 23 and 38 are shown in Fig. 7 . Average particle sizes for the fractions 5, 15 and 23 have been found to be 2.3(9), 1.9(7) and 1.1(4) nm, respectively. The size of the particles from fraction 38 was difficult to evaluate statistically due to the even smaller particle size and poor contrast on the images.

Based on the results of obtained high-resolution TEM data we hypothesise that separation of a carbon nanoparticles mixture in the desalting column takes place according to the principles of size-exclusion chromatography. The larger nanoparticles elute first because this particle type cannot penetrate though the beads of the column (fractions 5–22). In the next 23–37 fractions appears the intermediate CNDs that partly penetrate to the Sephadex beads. At the last fractions, the smallest nanoparticles easily enter the beads pores and they elute with the solvent molecules.

It is important to note that column size-exclusion chromatography method makes it possible to separate a mixture of carbon nanoparticles obtained by hydrothermal synthesis to the fractions that are more homogeneous in size and luminescent properties. This ability of particle size control is of crucial importance to a number of applications including biomedicine (e.g. development of fluorophores with tunable particle size and hence light absorption and emission), in solar cell sensitization (by tuning solar energy absorption range) and hydrogen separation (by tuning the HOMO-LUMO gap size) [38,39].

We have synthesized luminescent carbon nanoparticles in aqueous solution using natural polymer sodium dextran sulfate as template for hydrothermal carbonization. The emission of CNDs have a widely-reported excitation-dependent spectra. We found out that the PL intensity significantly depends on reabsorption effect due to the large number of different luminescent centers in water suspension of CNDs. In order to reveal the intrinsic PL there is a necessity to dilute CNDs water solutions to reduce a concentration of nanoparticles in the water solution.

We demonstrated an effective separation of CNDs using commercial gel chromatography column. The advantage of this purification and separation method of CNDs is the possibility of a detailed comparative analysis of different nanoparticles fractions formed within single synthesis. We determined that CNDs solution contains at least three types of carbon molecular structures. The first type shows short emitting maxima in the area around 410–420 nm, second type of nanoparticles has emitting maxima at a longer wavelength in the area of 490 nm. The third type of carbon nanoparticles has emitting maxima at around 530 nm. We thus demonstrated that hydrothermal synthesis results in polydispersed CNDs with distinctively different luminescent properties. Furthermore, these nanoparticles can be relatively easily separated into distinctive fractions for further studies using desalting columns.

These CNDs contain carboxylic acid groups, hydroxylic groups on their surface, which should allow for further functionalization with biomacromolecules. Given their low cost, ready scalability, excellent chemical stability, and special properties, the CNDs synthesized by this novel route should inspire research into the origin of their unique properties and encourage their exploration in a multitude of exciting areas, ranging from medical diagnostics to catalysis, photovoltaics, optical-power limiting, and optical-gain applications. All these envisaged applications require further understanding of the structure of CNDs and relationship between the structure and optical properties. We believe that the proposed method of separation provides a promising way towards achieving these goals and thus enabling practical applications of CNDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Russian Science foundation (project 16-13-10195).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.11.039.
